coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional bio-based photothermal hydrogel for highly efficient seawater desalination and contaminant adsorption
coredata.dc:creator: pattnayak, Bibek Chaw
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a vertically aligned porous photothermal PVA-psyllium husk hybrid hydrogel (PHH) has been prepared by introducing naturally abundant biomass psyllium husk (PH), along with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-iron oxide hybrid nanocomposite (NCQD@Fe3O4) into polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel. Using PHH, an evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 and ≈ 92% solar to vapor conversion efficiency have been achieved under 1 sun. After 10 cycling tests, PHH shows an evaporation rate of ⁓ 2.5–2.6 kg m−2 h−1 under different harsh conditions (pH 3–14 and salinity up to 250 g/kg), indicating long-lasting stability. In addition to this, the PHH evaporator shows excellent salt-resistant desalination and contaminant adsorption performance in seawater. Owing to a series of merits such as cost-effectiveness ($5.23/m2), high vapor conversion efficiency, excellent transport, and salt mitigation properties this renewable biomass-based evaporator may open new possibilities in the deployment of low-cost solar water purifiers with assured water quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQD@Fe3O4
Psyllium husk
Interfacial evaporation
Biomass
Cost-effective

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported SERB, DST, Govt. of India ( CRG/2018/001717 ). BC thanks DST-INSPIRE for providing fellowship ( IF190390 ). Authors thank DST-FIST ( SR/FTP/CS-I/2010/18 ) for providing BET surface area analyser.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the magnet-assisted synthesis of PHH to reduce the amount of solar absorber needed.

SEM images of PHHs having different contents of PH at various magnifications. (a–h) Longitudinal section of the (a) PHH1, (b) PHH2, (c) PHH3, and (d) PHH4 at low-resolution; (e) PHH1, (f) PHH2, (g) PHH3, and (h) PHH4 at higher resolution. (i-l) Cross-sectional SEM images of the (i) PHH1, (j) PHH2, (k) PHH3, and (l) PHH4 hydrogels.
N2 adsorption-desorption plots of the (a) PHH2, (b) PHH3, and (c) PHH4; pore size distribution curve of the (d) PHH2, (e) PHH3, (f) PHH4 hydrogels respectively.

(a) Comparative rheology study of PVA, PVA@PH, and PHH (b) Compressive stress-strain curve PHH1, PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4 hydrogels. (c) Density and stress of PHH1, PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4 hydrogels. (d) FTIR plot of pure PVA (black), pure PH (blue), and PHH (red). (e) UV-Vis-NIR spectra of PHH1, PHH2, and PHH3 hydrogels. (f) Thermal conductivity of dry PHH, wet PHH, and pure water at 40˚C.

(a) surface temperature changes of water, NCQD only, Fe3O4 only, NCQD: Fe3O4 (2:1), and NCQD: Fe3O4 (1:1) under the irradiation of 1000 W m−2. (b) The IR images of the interfacial temperature of NCQD only, Fe3O4 only, NCQD: Fe3O4 (2:1), and NCQD: Fe3O4 (1:1) (c) Surface temperature of PHHs with time. (d) The infrared images of PHH3 with time.

(a) Time-dependent mass change of PHHs. (b) The rate of evaporation and solar vapour conversion efficiency of PHHs for pure water under solar irradiation of 1 sun. (c) Comparison of mass change of water and corresponding evaporation enthalpy of various samples under dark conditions. (d) Fitting peaks of Raman spectra showing FW and IW in PHH3. (e) Time-lapse digital images of the salt dissolving on the top surface of the PHH evaporator.

(a) Digital image of lab-made solar desalination set-up for purified water collection upon solar irradiation. (b) Solar intensity and temp. vs time on a sunny day. (c) Water evaporation rate and collected purified water vs time. (d) Analysis of desalination performance of seawater sample before and after desalination. (e) Adsorption behavior of PHHs removal of heavy metal ions. f) Adsorption and removal of water-soluble organic dyes.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional bio-based photothermal hydrogel for highly efficient seawater desalination and contaminant adsorption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha 769008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, Odisha, INDIA-769008

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a vertically aligned porous photothermal PVA-psyllium husk hybrid hydrogel (PHH) has been prepared by introducing naturally abundant biomass psyllium husk (PH), along with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots-iron oxide hybrid nanocomposite (NCQD@Fe3O4) into polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel. Using PHH, an evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 and ≈ 92% solar to vapor conversion efficiency have been achieved under 1 sun. After 10 cycling tests, PHH shows an evaporation rate of ⁓ 2.5–2.6 kg m−2 h−1 under different harsh conditions (pH 3–14 and salinity up to 250 g/kg), indicating long-lasting stability. In addition to this, the PHH evaporator shows excellent salt-resistant desalination and contaminant adsorption performance in seawater. Owing to a series of merits such as cost-effectiveness ($5.23/m2), high vapor conversion efficiency, excellent transport, and salt mitigation properties this renewable biomass-based evaporator may open new possibilities in the deployment of low-cost solar water purifiers with assured water quality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh water is an indispensable requirement for living organisms. However, with the development of civilization, there is a continuous increase in water pollution as well as utilization of groundwater, which impose a serious scarcity of clean water [1–3]. To address this rising scarcity, eco-friendly strategies for clean water production from seawater have drawn enormous attention. More specifically solar energy-powered desalination has received heightened attraction as solar energy is superior in terms of availability, sustainability, and environmental benignity to all other fossil fuel-based energy sources [4–6]. A cost-effective, sunlight-driven water purifier with efficient conversion of solar energy to heat energy as well as a greater rate of pure water production is essential [7,8]. A myriad of solar thermal materials such as inorganic materials [9–14], organic [15–17], carbon-based materials [18–22], and conjugated polymer-based solar concentrators [23–26] have been designed to harvest the solar energy by converting it to heat to achieve productive water evaporation. However, at the same time, the use of expensive materials processed through tedious synthetic steps increases the overall cost of the technology [27–29]. Further, in most solar steam devices, rapid generation as well as localization of heat leads to the accumulation of salts which inevitably reduces light absorption by photothermal materials, increases the interfacial distance between seawater and the material, and thus reduces the steam generation [30–32]. The theoretical evaporation limit of a 2D evaporator is nearly 1.47 kg m−2 h−1. Whereas 3D hydrogels containing photothermal 2D nanostructures can significantly reduce the evaporation enthalpy by forming numerous hydrogen bonds between the hydrophilic group of polymer matrix and water molecules, thereby can achieve a better evaporation rate than that of the theoretical limit under the solar illumination of one sun. It is worth noting that a 3D evaporator developed by Xu et al. achieved an evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 [33]. Recently, Yuan et al. reported a 3D printed porous black hydrogel, but the evaporation performance was not impressive. They could achieve an evaporation rate of 1.33 kg m−2 h−1 [34]. Therefore, the development of photothermal floating material with low evaporation enthalpy, anti-salt accumulation properties, and low production cost needs urgent attention.

Recently hydrogel-based nanostructure, which is assembled by the 3D polymer network has achieved a record evaporation rate under one sun because of replenishment of H2O molecules through microchannels and internal pores of hydrogel evaporators [35,36]. The presence of a hydrophilic group on the polymer network can interact with the intermediate water molecules through hydrogen bonding by adjusting the states of water to facilitate the solar evaporation process using less amount of energy. More specifically, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) having abundant hydroxyl groups, micro gaps and internal small channels leads to the better transportation of water via hydrogen bonding and has been designed to harvest the solar energy by converting it to heat to achieve productive water evaporation. The water getting evaporated from the surface can be fulfilled by the internal pores of the PVA which leads to a continuous process [37]. Further, in a study governed by Yu et al., it has been established that PVA exhibits very low heat of localization to the surrounding bulk water, which is a very essential characteristic for a solar desalination probe to produce a better photothermal effect [38]. The self-cleaning property of PVA hydrogel ensures the long-term utility of the material [39–42]. Despite being the best floating material for solar desalination, the cost of the raw material and required technology for large-scale production is challenging for pilot-scale implementation. There has been a continuous effort to enhance the evaporation rate of PVA-based photothermal hydrogels. The incorporation of semiconductor nanomaterials in PVA-hydrogel is a popular technique to enhance the photothermal effect by nonradiative relaxation phenomena of lattice vibration [43]. Nonetheless, low-cost and simple fabrication processes are essential factors for their practical large-scale utilization. On the other hand, Carbon-dots are a unique class of carbon-based nanomaterials, that attract considerable scientific and technological interest because of their simple fabrication process, unique physiological properties, and environmental friendliness. Particularly, a C-dot containing nitrogen can shift the absorbance towards a higher wavelength and enhance the photothermal conversion efficiency [44]. Although NIR light harvesting properties of N-doped carbon dots (NCD) has been frequently explored for medical purpose, surprisingly, there are scanty reports on the utilization of NCD for solar water evaporation.

Taking all these facts into consideration, we aim to develop a cost-effective naturally available biomass-derived hybrid hydrogel (PHH) solar desalination device. We have used PVA along with renewable natural source psyllium husk (Plantago ovata) to synthesize a highly insulating porous floating material. Further, we have rationally incorporated the NCQD@Fe3O4 hybrid solar absorbers in the PHH to enhance the localization of heat on the hydrogel surface. PH helps to increase the hydration ability of the PHH and to build porous structure in PVA gel which makes the evaporation rate faster and lowers the vaporization enthalpy. Further, the presence of magnetic NCQD@Fe3O4 in the upper layer can control the movement of solar evaporators rationally to improve the evaporation rate. Using cheap raw material and fabrication methods, the production cost of our device comes down to $5.23/m2 with an evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 and ≈ 92% energy efficiency under one sun. Further due to abundant hydroxyl groups, PHH can bind to heavy metals and other organic contaminants to remove them from the polluted water.

Citric acid (CA), melamine (MA), and methylene blue (MB) were bought from Sigma Aldrich. Ferrous chloride (FeCl2.4 H2O) was obtained from Lobachemie Pvt. Ltd, India. Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6 H2O) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Merck. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) of M.W. 60,000–1,25,000 was purchased from Himedia. Psyllium husk (PH), amoxicillin (AMX, Alkem lab.ltd.), and ciprofloxacin (CPX, Neutec Healthcare Pvt. Ltd.) was bought from the local market of NIT Rourkela. Double distilled water was used during the experiment. All the materials were used without further purification.

NCQD@Fe3O4 was synthesized hydrothermally. At first, 0.176 g of FeCl2.4 H2O and 0.192 g of FeCl3.6 H2O were mixed with 20 mL of distilled water forming solution-A. Solution-B was made by mixing 0.126 g MA, 0.192 g CA, and 1 mL of HCl in a mixture solution of 30 mL of distilled water and 10 mL of ethanol. Solution A and B were put together in a round bottom flask and the mixture was stirred at 60 ℃ under an N2 atmosphere for 3 h. The pH of the mixture was then regulated to 10 by adding ammonium hydroxide. The black color solution thus obtained was stirred at 60 ℃ for the next 5 h. After completion, the obtained solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave with heating at 180 °C for 8 h. After the completion of the reaction, the solution was cooled and separated by using a magnetic bar. The resulting particles were freeze-dried to obtain NCQD@Fe3O4 and stored for further use.

PH was ground into fine particles and mixed with PVA with a weight ratio of 1:10. 6.6 gm of PH/PVA mixture was mixed with 60 mL of hot water and heated at 60 ℃ to form a uniform solution C. An aqueous dispersion of NCQD@Fe3O4 (3 mg/1 mL) was added to solution C with continuous stirring to make a uniform distribution of solar absorber in the hydrogel. The gelation process was done overnight and the beaker was kept on a magnet to concentrate NCQD@Fe3O4 at the bottom layer followed by soaking in distilled water overnight. The obtained hydrogel was frozen at − 20 ℃ and thawed in distilled water at 30 ℃. The freeze-thawing process was repeated 3 times and then freeze-dried. According to the different PH/PVA weight ratios of 0:1, 0.05:1, 0.1:1, and 0.15:1, the synthesized hydrogels were named PHH1, PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4 respectively.

The morphological study of NCQD@Fe3O4 and freeze-dried PHH were done by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL JSM- 6480 LV) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST). Surface functionalization was recorded using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu). The Raman spectra were characterized using a PL micro-Raman spectrometer (Witec Alpha300, Germany). The X-ray diffraction pattern was collected using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker AXS D8 Advance). The absorbance of the liquid samples was recorded with the help of a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600). For the analysis of absorption spectra, a UV–vis–NIR spectrometer having BaSO4 fine powder as a reference (Agilent Cary 5000, measured using an integrating sphere). The surface temperature and infrared images were captured using a Testo 868 infrared camera. Particle size was determined using Nano ZS90, Malvern instrument. A xenon lamp of 300 W was used as a simulated solar light source. The evaporation enthalpy and thermogram were analyzed using the Differential scanning calorimeter (NETZSC; Maia 200F3). The concentrations of different ions were studied by ICP-OES (inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy). The compressive stress-strain curves were obtained from the universal testing machine (Electroplus E1000 & E3000 test system).

The practical desalination activity of PHH hydrogels has been verified in seawater collected from Paradip port (the Bay of Bengal). A beaker filled with 100 mL of seawater and floating PHH was placed in a lab-made vapor condensation system. Upon irradiation of simulated sunlight, the evaporated desalinated water was deposited at the bottom of the condensation system. The desalinated water was collected from the bottom of the system for further salinity study.

The adsorption experiment was performed using an incubator shaker at a speed of 150 rpm taking methylene blue, ciprofloxacin, and amoxicillin as model contaminants. Initially, a batch of aqueous solutions having different contaminants was taken in a 100 mL beaker. PHH hydrogel was placed over the solution as displayed in Fig. 1 and shaken in an incubator for 12 h to achieve an adsorption equilibrium. The adsorption of MB and antibiotics was investigated from absorbance using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Adsorption of metal ions from a seawater sample was also verified using ICP-OES.

All the experiments were done in five replicates and the statistical significance of the entire data was analyzed using OriginPro software. One-way and two-way analyses of variance (ANOVA) having Tukey’s comparison were applied. All the determination values were expressed in terms of means ± SD and have been considered at p-value < 0.05 [40].

The schematic presentation of the hydrogel-based solar steam evaporator prototype is demonstrated in Fig. 1. The hybrid hydrogel PHH has been prepared by co-gelation of PH and PVA with NCQD@Fe3O4 as a solar-absorbing photothermal material. The TEM image shows the formation of NCQD@Fe3O4 nanocomposites of size in a range of 3–5 nm (Fig. S1). The NCQD appears slightly lighter and the Fe3O4 are a little darker in color, this is due to the lower electron density of NCQD than that of Fe3O4. The high-resolution TEM image clearly shows a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm and 0.25 nm, which can be attributed to the (002) graphitic plane of NCQD and (311) lattice plane of Fe3O4 respectively. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) data (Fig. S2) shows that the prepared NCQD@Fe3O4 possesses a hydrodynamic size of 30 nm which remains unchanged even after 30 days. The powder XRD of NCQD@Fe3O4 is presented in Fig. S3. The diffraction pattern of NCQD@Fe3O4 shows a small peak at 24.5°, which might be due to the (002) graphitic plane of NCQD [45]. The pattern also reveals the presence of magnetite (Fe3O4). As shown in the figure, the peaks at 30.5°, 35.69°, 37.04°, 43.63°, 54.17°, 57.56o, and 63.15° are assigned to (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of Fe3O4 respectively [46]. Additionally, the intrinsic structure of NCQD@Fe3O4 was further investigated with the help of Raman spectroscopy. In the spectrum (Fig. S4), the peak at 701 cm−1 is due to the vibrational mode of the Fe-O bond. At the same time, there are two fundamental bands for carbon also present in the spectrum. A characteristic D band formed at ≈ 1368 cm−1 is assigned to the sp3 defects, while the other characteristic G band at ≈ 1567 cm−1 is assigned to the planar vibrations of graphitic sp2 carbon atom and coincides with the E2g modes of graphitic carbon. The ratio of the two assigned characteristic peaks (ID/IG) is found to be 0.93, which indicates the graphitic structure of the synthesized product with some defects. The synthesis of PHH was accomplished by a three-step method. Subsequently, to induce secondary cross-linking, the hydrogel was freeze-thawed three times and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain a porous structure. Obtained PHH hydrogel remains stable for an extended period without any sedimentation due to very strong hydrogen bonding between the PVA, PH, and NCQD@Fe3O4. Synthesized PHHs can be modified easily into various forms by using moulds of various shapes before freeze-thawing. The adjustable spongy structure is an essential condition for the utility of hydrogel in desalination techniques. By varying the PH amount in PHH, the pores of the hydrogel can be easily controlled. As demonstrated in Fig. 2a-d, the SEM images of freeze-dried samples of different PHHs, indicate that the freeze-dried PHH1 sample doesn’t have an internal pore structure. Whereas, with the increase in PH amount, the crosslinked open-pore structure having different pore sizes have been developed, which is due to the limiting lateral expansion of ice crystals during the uni-directional freeze-drying process. The porous structures make possible the easy transportation of water through the capillary rise and supply enough water during the solar-driven interfacial evaporation process (section S1 in the supporting information) [47]. To gain further insight into pore channel size, high-resolution images were taken. As exhibited in Fig. 2e-h, the channel width of PHHs was found to be about 54 ± 1.21 µm for PHH2, 37 ± 2.12 µm for PHH3, and 13 ± 0.17 µm for PHH4 respectively. From the cross-sectional SEM images of the PHHs (Fig. 2i-l, S5), the pore sizes were found to be 25 ± 2.17 µm for PHH2, 15 ± 1.18 µm for PHH3, and 5 ± 1.77 µm for PHH4 respectively. In comparison to previously published papers on biomass derived hydrogels such as PVA/KGM [7] and PVA/Kaolin [41], where the pore size is limited to 25–30 µm, in our case, the pore structure of the PHHs could be well tuned to 15 µm by varying the PH amount. Such a porous gel with narrow pores not only enhances capillary rise but also promotes the absorption of light by minimizing the scattering of light [48]. Fig. S6 demonstrates the morphology and the presence of the synthesized NCQD@Fe3O4 inside the PHH. It is noteworthy that, the 3D porous structures of PHH not only enhance the solar absorbance but also encourage the easy transportation of water and easy escape from the water-air interface as described later. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping pictures confirmed the spatial distribution of iron, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms at the top side of the PHH (Fig. S6). Deposition of photothermal absorber NCQD@Fe3O4 on the evaporation surface enhances vapor conversion efficiency.

N2 adsorption-desorption study was conducted to investigate the porosity of PHH hydrogel. The distribution of pore size was studied using Barrett-Joyner-Hallender (BJH) method. In the case of all hybrid hydrogels (PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4), type IV hysteresis loops were observed in the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm ( Fig. 3a-c) [49]. A continuous distribution of mesoporous and microporous pores, which is invariable with the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm has been observed in all cases (Fig. 3d-f). The pore volumes were found to be 0.32 cm3 g−1 for PHH2, 0.45 cm3 g−1 for PHH3, and 0.57 cm3 g−1 for PHH4 respectively. Using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, the specific surface areas were calculated to be 31.01 m2 g−1 for PHH2, 53.23 m2 g−1 for PHH3, and 58.34 m2 g−1 for PHH4 respectively.

A series of shear stress loop tests were conducted to understand the effect of PH on the mechanical strength of PVA ( Fig. 4a). Hydrogel containing only PVA sample was considered as a control sample. Both hydrogels containing only PVA and PH (PVA@PH) and PHH have greater shear stress values than pure PVA. By introducing the PH component in PVA, the shear stress values increased two times that of pure PVA hydrogel, indicating better mechanical strength of hybrid hydrogel. Again, the introduction of NCQD@Fe3O4 in the hybrid hydrogel also enhances the shear stress value more than that of PVA and PVA@PH hydrogel, representing better stability of PHH. The enhanced stability of PHH is due to the better cross-linking by hydrogen bonding, which is produced due to the greater surface-functionalization of the NCQD and the low-stress value is due to the low concentration of crosslinker or no crosslinker [50]. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of PHHs were studied through a compression test by the use of the universal testing machine. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the compression strengths of PHHs were found to be 0.195, 0.215, 0.245, and 0.325 MPa for PHH1, PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4 respectively. Additionally, the density of PHH hydrogels also increased from 1.29 to 1.49 g cm−3 with the increase in PH amount (Fig. 4c). The above results demonstrated that PH and NCQD@Fe3O4 play a crucial role in enhancing the mechanical strength and stability of in the PHH hydrogel by inducing greater cross-linking in the structure. The presence of functional groups in PHH was studied using the FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 4d). In case of PH the peaks at 1049, 1625, 1720 cm−1 are associated with C-O-C stretching of ether, -C-O stretching frequency of hydroxyl and -CO stretching of carbonyl groups respectively [51]. For PVA, the peak at 1095 cm−1 indicates the stretching frequency of -C-O-C. In case of PHH, the broad peak at 3180 cm−1 matches with the -OH stretching frequency indicating the establishment of strong hydrogen bonding among the -OH groups (3121 cm−1) of PH and -OH group (3225 cm−1) of PVA [7]. PHH curve includes the peaks of PH and PVA, certifying the polymerized hybrid network.

We postulate that NCQD@Fe3O4 has a better photothermal behavior due to two combined effects; the extra Fe3O4 reinforces the absorbance of the system and at the same time reduces the heat loss. To confirm that, we first carried out the absorption studies of NCQD@Fe3O4 and only NCQD. Fig. S7 demonstrates that there is a 2-fold enhancement in the broadband light absorbance of NCQD@Fe3O4 than that of only NCQD. The enhancement is due to the interfacial nanocavity among the NCQD and Fe3O4 layers, which intensifies the absorbance of light with minimum transmittance and reflectance across the solar spectrum up to 2500 nm. Additionally, the unique textured Fe3O4 coating reduces the reflection, leading to the enhancement in light concentration on the NCQD surface instead of being reflected away, a key factor for better solar-heat conversion. Besides, PHH hydrogel possessed a broad light absorbance in a range of (200–2500 nm).

As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the average absorption of the PHHs is (97−98) %. Around ≈ (2−3) % absorption loss is because of the reflectance and there is nearly 0% transmittance (Fig. S8). The transmittance and reflectance were calculated by using the absorption formula (1-T-R). Secondly, the low thermal conductivity of PHH is necessary to block the dissipation of heat to the encircling water. The thermal conductivity of dry PHHs was found to be 0.23 W m−1 K−1, which is considerably lower than that of pure water (0.6 W m−1 K−1) (section S5 in the supporting information). On the other hand, water undergoes penetration into the pores of the synthesized PHHs during the desalination process, therefore enhancing the thermal conductivity process by destructing the thermal insulation performance. The thermal conductivity of the wet state PHH is found to be 0.28 W m−1 K−1, which is not as high as compared to the dry PHHs (Fig. 4f). In this solar-vapor interfacial evaporation process, PHH with a lower thermal conductivity value can significantly reduce the loss of heat to bulk surrounding water. To further conclude the suppressed heat loss effect and the dependence of the photothermal effect on the concentration of Fe3O4, an analysis was done by fabricating NCQD with different concentrations of Fe3O4. PHH with different concentrations of Fe3O4 were irradiated with simulated sunlight of intensity 1 kW m−2 and the temperature of the evaporation surface was measured using IR thermal camera. From Fig. 5a-b, it was observed that PHH having NCQD@Fe3O4 reaches a maximum temperature of 48 ℃ with the increase in Fe3O4 concentration (from 0% to 100%). A control experiment using only NCQD results in a much lower equilibrium temperature of 37.7 ℃, ≈ 10 ℃ lower than that of NCQD@Fe3O4, and also there was no obvious photoheating performance observed from PHH with only Fe3O4. There was a slight increase in temperature of both pure NCQD and Fe3O4 after 60 min, but in case of NCQD@Fe3O4, there was a significant increase in surface temperature indicating the anti-reflectance of NCQD@Fe3O4 originated because of surface coverage of NCQD by Fe3O4. The photothermal property of PHHs was analyzed by marking the temperature of the PHH surface and the bottom water under the illumination of one sun. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the temperature of the hydrogel surface and underlying water was unaffected by changing the PH amount in PHH. However, the surface temperature of all PHHs increases for the first 50 min and reaches equilibrium at 46⁓47 ℃ after 60 min, whereas the temperature of the bottom water is at 33⁓34.5 ℃. In case of PHH3, the surface attains a higher temperature of 47 ℃ and the lower bottom temperature of 34.4 ℃ due to the lower thermal conductivity (Fig. 5d).

One benefit of choosing hydrogel as a solar evaporator is its better water transportation ability from the bottom layer to the upper surface and greater water content. Here, PHH with the same concentration of solar absorber NCQD@Fe3O4 (3 mg mL−1) and various PH/PVA weight ratios were synthesized. With the increase in the weight of PH in the hydrogel, the inhomogeneous co-gelation arose and lead to the form of a hybrid hydrogel [7]. Besides, PH can absorb many times its weight in water medium and forms a very high viscous suspension [52]. PH itself has swelling capability, so different weight ratios of PH in PVA impact the swelling behavior of PHHs, water transportability, and evaporation performance. The saturated water content of PHH showed a significant statistical difference concerning only PVA (PHH1). The value increases with an increase in the PH amount in the gel indicating that PH can control the swelling capacity of PHHs (Fig. S9). Simultaneously, the time needed to achieve the fully swollen stage from the squeezed half-swollen state reduces with the rise in PH amount and also the transport rate of water (saturated content of water/time for half-swollen) increases from 0.05 g min−1 to 0.16 g min−1 with the increase in PH/PVA ratio to 0.1:1 and then decreased. The solar-vapor conversion rate of PHH was quantitatively studied under the illumination of simulated solar light of one sun. The change in mass of evaporated water was studied by using an electronic balance. From Fig. 6a, it was observed that with the increase in time, the mass of distillate was increased and the value was highest for PHH3, which was much higher than that of normal water. PHH3 displayed a higher evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1, which is 4 times higher than that of water (0.65 kg m−2 h−1) (Fig. 6b). The solar-vapor conversion efficiency of PHHs was calculated by using equation-1 (section S3 in the supporting information) and found to be 92% for PHH3, which is better than the other solar absorbers. The ultrafast evaporation rate and higher evaporation efficiency are due to the remarkable function of PHH in reducing the required energy for water evaporation. According to the molecular interaction of water molecules with the polymer matrix, the hydrogel polymer matrix includes three water states such as free water, bound water, and intermediate water. Among those water states, intermediate water is the main water form which demands very less energy to get away from the evaporation surface by decreasing its water vaporization enthalpy [53]. In PHH, PH plays a crucial role in controlling the water states and in reducing the water evaporation enthalpy. To confirm the role of PH on water transportability and in the reduction of evaporation enthalpy of water, an evaporation test was conducted under dark conditions (Fig. 6c and section S4 in the supporting information). The corresponding evaporation enthalpies of PHHs are found to be 1400, 1336, 1273, and 1324 J/g for PHH1, PHH2, PHH3, and PHH4 respectively. The trend of calculated evaporation enthalpy was well-matched with DSC results (Fig. S12 and Table-S1, supporting information). Surprisingly, the water evaporation enthalpy of PHH3 was quite lower than that of normal water (2450 J/g), which implied that by utilizing the same amount of energy more water could be evaporated. Such a low value of evaporation enthalpy could be assigned to the presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups in the PHH matrix and the development of a porous structure. As demonstrated in Fig. 2, the aligned vertical structure induces capillary rise, which can enhance the hydrophilicity to increase water transportation by generating additional upward capillary pressure. In the case of PHH4, with the increase in PH amount more cross-linked connected structures are generated between the interconnected channels, which weakened the capillary effect and decreased the transportation rate of water. Further, we analyzed the Raman spectra in the O-H stretching region to display the hydrogen bonding of water molecules in all PHHs, indicating the water state in the PHH. The peaks at 3231 and 3403 cm−1 correspond to FW (Fig. 6d) having four hydrogen bonds (two protons and two lone electron pairs are involved in hydrogen bonding with the adjacent water molecules), while the peaks at 3518 and 3632 cm−1 are associated with weakly hydrogen-bonded IW (Fig. 6d) [54]. Hence, the stronger IW peaks compared with FW peaks indicate a higher proportion of IW in the PHH. It has been observed that with the increase

in the psyllium husk ratio, the ratio of IW/FW increases from 0.75(PHH1) to 1.11(PHH3) and then decreases to 1.07 (PHH4) (Fig.S13). Based on the above result, the water transmission rate and the evaporation enthalpy are well-balanced to an extent in case of PHH3, so PHH3 having the most IW shows a better evaporation rate and evaporation efficiency, which is comparable to or higher than that of the others reported earlier (Table S2, supporting information). Besides, mitigation of accumulated salt on the evaporator surface is a challenge for better solar-driven interfacial evaporation. In PHHs, the NaCl crystals were ejected in 60 min after being loaded on the exterior surface, which confirms the salt self-cleaning property of PHHs (Fig. 6e). Additionally, the solar desalination study was also performed using seawater of salinity ranges 0–350 g/kg under simulated sunlight of intensity 1000 W m−2. As depicted in Fig. S14, the evaporation efficiency of PHH3 was unchanged up to a salinity range of 250 g/kg and decreased with a further increase in salinity. It is noteworthy that, PHH3 shows excellent salt-resistant properties owing to the combined effect of superhydrophilicity as well as the vertically aligned porous channel. The consistency in the performance of PHH3 was further evaluated by measuring the evaporation rate of PHH3 in different harsh environments under simulated solar light of intensity 1 Sun. From Fig. S15, it is found that PHH showed a constant evaporation rate. PHH3 is also found to perform well in interfacial evaporation in a wide pH range of 3–12 (Fig. S16), which usually covers all types of water. As shown in Fig. S17, the evaporation rate of seawater under one sun remains consistent as ⁓ 2.5 kg m−2 h−1 for a period of 90 h. Surprisingly, after 10 cycling tests, PHH3 showed a stable evaporation rate in different harsh conditions, which reflected the consistency and anti-salt accumulation property of PHH3 over a wide range of salinity and pH (Fig. S18). This anti-salt-fouling ability of hydrogel is because of the equilibrium established between the salt absorption and diffusion-based salt discharge. Therefore, a constant low concentration of salt accumulated inside the hydrogel prevents the crystallization of solid salts [39]. These results indicated that 3D hydrogel-based evaporator can be used for a continuous ZLD saline water treatment up to a salinity range of 250 g/kg, which is quite realistic since the average salinity of seawater is 35 g/kg.

To assess the outdoor solar evaporation and desalination performance, a laboratory-designed water collection system has been used where the steam is condensed and the desalinated water was collected as displayed in Fig. 7a. The outdoor experiment of PHH3 was performed from 9:00–18:00 under the natural sunlight by careful measuring of solar intensity, surrounding temperature and change in mass of the system. As can be noted in Fig. 7b, the change in ambient temperature followed the trend of change in solar intensity. During the experiment, the highest temperature of 37.5°C was recorded at 14:00. The PHH evaporator exhibits the highest evaporation rate of 2.7 kg m−2 h−1 at 14:00 and agreed with the change in solar intensity (Fig. 7c). After outdoor solar evaporation of 10 h, the PHH evaporator generated evaporated water of amount 20.9 kg m−2, which is enough for the daily water intake of seven individuals. To examine the potential application of PHH in desalination, we measured the concentrations of major ions such as Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+ in the distillate purified from real seawater. From inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), it was found that there was ≈ 10,000-fold decrease in the concentrations of fundamental ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+) in the distillate compared to the original seawater. For instance, the Na+ concentration falls from 10,400 mg L−1 to 10.6 mg L−1, which is less than the permissible limit of Na+ in drinking water as defined by WHO (World Health Organisation) (Fig. 7d) [55].

Interestingly, PHH exhibits a good adsorption property towards the dissolved organic and inorganic impurities in seawater. The concentration of metal ions (Zn2+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ in ppb) in the water was reduced by one hundred to two hundred parts after incubating PHH in seawater for 4 h (Fig. 7e). The addition of PH to the PVA gel forms a hybrid polymer network and the chelation is more favored due to the excess of -OH group in the PH component [56]. Metal ions having vacant outer n s and (n-1) d orbitals can readily accept the extra electron pair from oxygen and can form a chelate bond with the abundant –OH groups easily. When PVA@PH hydrogel was incubated with an aqueous of MB for 2 h, the blue color of MB solution was gradually decolorized (Fig. 7f) and the white color of the PVA@PH hydrogel was turned blue, demonstrating easy adsorption of MB on the porous 3D network of PHH (Fig. S19). The adsorption may be attributed to the chemisorption of MB driven by hydrogen bonding interaction between the –OH groups of PHH and nitrogen/oxygen atoms of organic dye. In an alkaline medium, the deprotonation of –OH groups may further trigger the adsorption of MB-type cationic dyes [57]. Among heavy metal ions, Hg2+ has a special evaporative property, which makes it quite difficult to remove by a simple distillation process [58]. Fig. 7e shows the concentration of Hg2+ was decreased by four parts of magnitude in the supernatant due to the adsorption behavior of PHH.

Further, the adsorption of antibiotics such as AMX and CPX was studied by shaking PHH with antibiotic-containing water for 24 h. Fig. S20 shows that the AMX removal rate was 100% after 12 h, whereas only 45% of CPX was adsorbed even after 24 h (Fig. S21). The adsorption is ascribed to the greater hydrogen bonding between the antibiotic and PHH. The porous structure of prepared PHH also supports better adsorption of a wide range of contaminants [59]. The adsorption property of a hydrogel-based evaporator is a primary requirement to enhance the quality of the purified water for multipurpose use like drinking water, household work, and agriculture. The altogether purification efficiency of PHH shows its outstanding potential for the heavy metal removal and waste-water purification process.

In a nutshell, we have fabricated a mechanically strong photothermal hydrogel for efficient solar-driven interfacial evaporation by judiciously incorporating different components in PVA hydrogel. A coating of Fe3O4 on NCQD significantly improves the photothermal property and the incorporation of NCQD@Fe3O4 in insulating PHH hydrogel significantly amplifies the photothermal conversion efficiency. PHHs are demonstrated to exhibit a faster water evaporation rate of 2.6 kg m−2 h−1 with ≈ 92% solar-vapor conversion efficiency under one sun irradiation in a wide pH range. The cheap material PH boosts the hydration ability of hydrogel for water transport and contributes toward the reduction of water evaporation enthalpy and removal of organic and inorganic pollutants. Owing to its affordable cost, long-term stability, and outstanding performance in solar evaporation, PHH may have promising applications in the production of drinkable water, metal extraction, antibiotic removal, and wastewater treatment.

Bibek Chaw pattnayak is responsible for all experimental work, data analysis and original draft of the manuscript. Dr. S. Mohapatra is responsible for conceptualization, writing, review and correction of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Architecting highly hydratable polymer networks to tune the water state for solar water purification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhou, F. Zhao, Y. Guo, B. Rosenberger, G. Yu, Architecting highly hydratable polymer networks to tune the water state for solar water purification, Science advances, 5 (2019) eaaw5484.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: W.H. Organization, Safe drinking-water from desalination, World Health Organization, 2011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ligand design for selective complexation of metal ions in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.D. Hancock, A.E. Martell, Ligand design for selective complexation of metal ions in aqueous solution, Chemical Reviews, 89 (1989) 1875–1914.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient removal of methylene blue dye by adsorption onto carbon dot @ zinc peroxide embedded poly vinyl alcohol (PVA/CZnO2) nano-composite: a novel Reusable adsorbent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. El-Shamy, An efficient removal of methylene blue dye by adsorption onto carbon dot @ zinc peroxide embedded poly vinyl alcohol (PVA/CZnO2) nano-composite: A novel Reusable adsorbent, Polymer, 202 (2020) 122565.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic high‐rate solar steaming and mercury removal with MoS2/C@ polyurethane composite sponges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, M.C. Tekell, Y. Huang, K. Bertelsmann, M. Lau, D. Fan, Synergistic High‐Rate Solar Steaming and Mercury Removal with MoS2/C@ Polyurethane Composite Sponges, Advanced Energy Materials, 8 (2018) 1802108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of a three-dimensional porous graphene oxide-kaolinite-poly(vinyl alcohol) composite for efficient adsorption and removal of ciprofloxacin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Huang, J. Tian, Y. Li, X. Yin, W. Wu, Preparation of a Three-Dimensional Porous Graphene Oxide-Kaolinite-Poly(vinyl alcohol) Composite for Efficient Adsorption and Removal of Ciprofloxacin, Langmuir, 36 (2020) 10895–10904.
